1→# Building a macOS App in Rust with GPUI
     2→
     3→> A field guide to the essential primitives, dependencies, and architectural decisions
     4→> required before writing your first line of application code.
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## Prologue: What GPUI Is (and Isn't)
     9→
    10→GPUI is not a cross-platform widget toolkit. It is not a WebView wrapper. It is not
    11→AppKit with a Rust skin. It is **Zed's internal application framework** — a
    12→Metal-backed, entity-ownership-driven UI engine that erases the macOS platform layer
    13→entirely, giving you a single Rust-native model for windows, layout, rendering, and
    14→input.
    15→
    16→The consequence of that design choice is significant: you never write Objective-C, never
    17→touch Cocoa, never reach for `winit` or `glutin`. You write `Entity<T>`, implement
    18→`Render`, call `cx.notify()`, and GPUI handles the rest.
    19→
    20→The trade-off is equally significant: GPUI is **not a stable public API**. It is an
    21→internal tool published openly because Zed itself is open source. It breaks on upstream
    22→changes without notice. Pin your dependency to a specific commit and treat forward-ports
    23→as a recurring maintenance cost.
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→## Act I: The Absolute Minimum
    28→
    29→### The Dependency
    30→
    31→GPUI is not published to crates.io. You pull it directly from the Zed monorepo as a git
    32→dependency:
    33→
    34→```toml
    35→[dependencies]
    36→gpui = { git = "https://github.com/zed-industries/zed", rev = "<commit-sha>" }
    37→```
    38→
    39→Pin the `rev`. Never track `main` in a working project.
    40→
    41→### The Entry Point
    42→
    43→The entire macOS run loop is three lines:
    44→
    45→```rust
    46→fn main() {
    47→    App::new().run(|cx| {
    48→        cx.open_window(WindowOptions::default(), |window, cx| {
    49→            cx.new(|cx| MyRootView::new(window, cx))
    50→        });
    51→    });
    52→}
    53→```
    54→
    55→`App::new()` boots the Metal renderer, initializes the Taffy layout engine, and
    56→configures the Cocoa event loop. `.run()` hands control to the platform and never
    57→returns. Everything your application does happens inside that closure or in entities
    58→spawned from it.
    59→
    60→### The Root View Contract
    61→
    62→Every renderable type implements `Render`:
    63→
    64→```rust
    65→pub trait Render: 'static + Sized {
    66→    fn render(&mut self, window: &mut Window, cx: &mut Context<Self>) -> impl IntoElement;
    67→}
    68→```
    69→
    70→Your root view is just a struct that implements this trait. There is no component
    71→lifecycle, no mount/unmount hooks, no virtual DOM. When GPUI needs a frame, it calls
    72→`render()`. When your state changes, you call `cx.notify()` to schedule the next frame.
    73→That is the entire contract.
    74→
    75→---
    76→
    77→## Act II: What GPUI Provides for Free
    78→
    79→You do not wire these up. They come with the dependency.
    80→
    81→### Metal-Backed Renderer
    82→
    83→GPUI owns a Metal render pipeline. Scene construction, GPU resource management, and
    84→frame submission are handled internally. You compose elements; GPUI draws them. You
    85→never touch a shader or a command buffer.
    86→
    87→### Taffy Layout Engine
    88→
    89→Layout is computed by **Taffy**, a pure-Rust implementation of CSS Flexbox and Grid.
    90→The API is Tailwind-inspired:
    91→
    92→```rust
    93→h_flex()                    // display: flex; flex-direction: row
    94→    .gap_2()                // gap: 0.5rem
    95→    .p_4()                  // padding: 1rem
    96→    .child(label)
    97→    .child(button)
    98→```
    99→
   100→`div()`, `h_flex()`, and `v_flex()` are your universal building blocks. Everything
   101→composes.
   102→
   103→### Core Text Integration
   104→
   105→Font loading, shaping, subpixel rendering, and line layout run through Core Text.
   106→You supply font data (via assets — see Act III); GPUI handles the rest.
   107→
   108→### Input Handling
   109→
   110→Keyboard events, mouse events, trackpad gestures, scroll, drag-and-drop — all
   111→normalized and delivered through GPUI's event system. You attach handlers with
   112→`.on_click()`, `.on_key_down()`, `.on_drag()`, `.on_drop()`. The platform
   113→differences between input devices are invisible to your code.
   114→
   115→### Accessibility
   116→
   117→GPUI constructs a basic macOS accessibility tree automatically from your element
   118→hierarchy. No manual `AXUIElement` wrangling required.
   119→
   120→---
   121→
   122→## Act III: What You Wire Up Yourself
   123→
   124→These are not optional. Without them, you have a black window.
   125→
   126→### Asset Loading
   127→
   128→GPUI requires an implementation of `AssetSource` to resolve fonts, icons, and images
   129→by path. The standard approach is to bake assets into the binary at compile time using
   130→`rust-embed`:
   131→
   132→```rust
   133→#[derive(RustEmbed)]
   134→#[folder = "assets/"]
   135→struct Assets;
   136→
   137→impl AssetSource for Assets {
   138→    fn load(&self, path: &str) -> Result<Option<Cow<[u8]>>> {
   139→        Self::get(path)
   140→            .map(|f| Some(f.data))
   141→            .ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("asset not found: {path}"))
   142→    }
   143→
   144→    fn list(&self, path: &str) -> Result<Vec<SharedString>> {
   145→        Ok(Self::iter()
   146→            .filter(|p| p.starts_with(path))
   147→            .map(SharedString::from)
   148→            .collect())
   149→    }
   150→}
   151→
   152→App::new().with_assets(Assets).run(|cx| { ... });
   153→```
   154→
   155→You need at minimum **one font embedded** in your assets. GPUI has no fallback system
   156→font loader. Without a font, nothing renders.
   157→
   158→### A Color / Theme System
   159→
   160→GPUI's styling primitives (`.bg()`, `.text_color()`, `.border_color()`) take `Hsla`
   161→or `Rgba` values directly. There is no built-in theme engine. Zed's `theme` crate
   162→exists but is enormous and tightly coupled to the full Zed workspace.
   163→
   164→Start minimal:
   165→
   166→```rust
   167→pub struct Theme {
   168→    pub background:       Hsla,
   169→    pub surface:          Hsla,
   170→    pub text_primary:     Hsla,
   171→    pub text_muted:       Hsla,
   172→    pub accent:           Hsla,
   173→    pub border:           Hsla,
   174→}
   175→
   176→impl Global for Theme {}
   177→```
   178→
   179→Register it with `cx.set_global(Theme::default())` at startup. Access it anywhere with
   180→`cx.global::<Theme>()`. Grow the palette as requirements demand.
   181→
   182→### The Global Pattern for App-Wide State
   183→
   184→Anything that isn't owned by a specific entity but needs to be accessible from any
   185→context — settings, theme, keybinding registry, feature flags — lives as a `Global`:
   186→
   187→```rust
   188→cx.set_global(Settings::default());
   189→cx.set_global(Theme::default());
   190→
   191→// Accessible anywhere cx: &App is in scope:
   192→let theme = cx.global::<Theme>();
   193→```
   194→
   195→Establish your globals in the `App::run()` closure, before opening any windows. This
   196→is the correct initialization order.
   197→
   198→### Keybinding Dispatch
   199→
   200→GPUI ships a full action dispatch system, but nothing is preconfigured. You define
   201→action types with the `actions!` macro, bind keys explicitly, and attach handlers with
   202→`.on_action()`:
   203→
   204→```rust
   205→actions!(my_app, [Quit, OpenFile, Save]);
   206→
   207→cx.bind_keys([
   208→    KeyBinding::new("cmd-q", Quit, None),
   209→    KeyBinding::new("cmd-o", OpenFile, None),
   210→    KeyBinding::new("cmd-s", Save, None),
   211→]);
   212→
   213→// In render():
   214→div()
   215→    .on_action(cx.listener(|this, _: &Save, window, cx| {
   216→        this.save(window, cx);
   217→    }))
   218→```
   219→
   220→The `key_context` string on your root element controls which action scope is active.
   221→
   222→---
   223→
   224→## Act IV: The Practical Dependency Surface
   225→
   226→Beyond GPUI itself, these are the crates you will need before the project is
   227→non-trivial.
   228→
   229→| Purpose | Crate | Why |
   230→|---|---|---|
   231→| Bake assets into binary | `rust-embed` | Required for font + icon loading |
   232→| Async executor | `smol` | GPUI's executor wraps smol; zero friction with `cx.spawn()` |
   233→| Serialization | `serde` + `serde_json` | State persistence, config, IPC |
   234→| Error handling | `anyhow` | Standard in the Zed ecosystem |
   235→| Ordered tree collections | `sum_tree` | Already in Zed; essential for virtualized list data |
   236→| Compact key-value persistence | `heed` or `rocksdb` | Panel state, user preferences |
   237→
   238→GPUI uses `smol` as its internal async executor. Using `smol` for your own background
   239→tasks means `cx.background_spawn(async { ... })` works without any runtime
   240→configuration.
   241→
   242→---
   243→
   244→## Act V: What You Never Touch
   245→
   246→Because GPUI fully abstracts the platform, these are genuinely off the table:
   247→
   248→- **AppKit / Cocoa** — hidden behind GPUI's platform layer
   249→- **Core Graphics** — GPUI's scene system writes directly to Metal
   250→- **Core Animation** — GPUI owns the display link
   251→- **Objective-C runtime** — no `objc` crate, no `#[objc_method]`
   252→- **`winit` or `glutin`** — GPUI has its own platform window implementation
   253→- **Any JavaScript runtime or WebView** — not in the model
   254→
   255→If you find yourself reaching for any of these, the architectural answer is almost
   256→always to push the work into a `cx.background_spawn()` task and surface the result
   257→back through an entity update.
   258→
   259→---
   260→
   261→## Act VI: The Sharp Edges
   262→
   263→### Platform Support
   264→
   265→GPUI targets **macOS (Metal)** and **Linux (Blade / Vulkan)**. Windows support exists
   266→in the repository but is incomplete. Plan for macOS as the primary target.
   267→
   268→### API Stability
   269→
   270→There is none. GPUI is Zed's internal framework. The context system already underwent
   271→one major refactor (the `ViewContext<T>` → `Context<T>` + `Window` split). The render
   272→trait signature changed. Entity creation APIs changed. This will happen again.
   273→
   274→The mitigation strategy: pin to a commit, do not auto-update, and schedule
   275→forward-port work deliberately rather than reactively.
   276→
   277→### The Mental Model Shift
   278→
   279→The most common early mistake is bringing expectations from React, SwiftUI, or other
   280→retained-mode frameworks. There is no reconciler, no component tree diffing, no
   281→`useEffect`. The model is:
   282→
   283→1. State lives in `Entity<T>` structs owned by `App`.
   284→2. Rendering is a pure function of that state, called every dirty frame.
   285→3. State changes happen through `entity.update(cx, |state, cx| { ... })`.
   286→4. Re-renders are scheduled by calling `cx.notify()`.
   287→5. Cross-entity reactivity is wired through `cx.observe()` and `cx.subscribe()`.
   288→
   289→Once this mental model is fully internalized, the platform layer disappears. You think
   290→in entities, subscriptions, and render functions — and GPUI handles everything below
   291→that.
   292→
   293→---
   294→
   295→## Epilogue: The Initialization Checklist
   296→
   297→Before your first meaningful frame renders, you need:
   298→
   299→- [ ] GPUI pinned to a specific commit in `Cargo.toml`
   300→- [ ] At minimum one font embedded via `rust-embed` and served by `AssetSource`
   301→- [ ] A `Theme` struct registered as a `Global`
   302→- [ ] A root entity implementing `Render` returned from `cx.open_window()`
   303→- [ ] At least one `actions!` block with keybindings registered
   304→- [ ] An async executor available for background work (`smol`)
   305→
   306→Everything else — panels, virtualized lists, reactive subscriptions, split-view
   307→layouts — builds on top of these foundations using the patterns described in this guide.

**Git Abstraction Module** (`src/git/`):
The entire git abstraction layer is implemented with 8 submodules:

1. **`types.rs`** (276 lines) - Core data types:
   - `RepoPath` - Path relative to repo root (Arc<Path> wrapper)
   - `FileStatus` (enum) - Added, Modified, Deleted, Untracked, Unchanged
   - `GitFileStatus` - Index + worktree status composite
   - `StagingState` - Staged, Unstaged, PartiallyStaged
   - `StatusEntry` - File path + status
   - `Branch` - Branch info with upstream tracking
   - `BlameEntry` - Blame line ranges with author/timestamp
   - `CommitSummary` & `CommitDetails` - Commit metadata
   - `PushOptions`, `CommitOptions`, `StashEntry`, `ConflictSide`
   - Includes comprehensive tests for path ordering, display, equality, and staging states

2. **`error.rs`** (30 lines) - Error handling:
   - `GitError` enum (thiserror-based):
     - `Git2Error`, `IoError`, `CliError`, `RepoNotFound`, `PathNotFound`, `InvalidOperation`, `MergeConflict`
   - `GitResult<T>` type alias

3. **`repository.rs`** (117 lines) - The core trait:
   - `GitRepository` trait with ~40 methods across:
     - Identity: `path()`, `work_directory()`
     - Staging/Index: `stage_paths()`, `unstage_paths()`, `set_index_text()`, `reload_index()`
     - Commit: `commit()`, `uncommit()`
     - Remote ops: `push()`, `pull()`, `fetch()`, `create_remote()`
     - Status: `status()`, `status_for_path()`
     - Branch ops: `current_branch()`, `branches()`, `create_branch()`, `checkout()`, `delete_branch()`, `merge_base()`, `remote_url()`
     - Diff/Content: `head_text_for_path()`, `index_text_for_path()`
     - Blame: `blame_for_path()`
     - Stash: `stash_list()`, `stash_all()`, `stash_pop()`, `stash_apply()`, `stash_drop()`
     - History: `log()`, `show()`
     - Conflict: `checkout_conflict_path()`

4. **`real_repo.rs`** (839 lines) - Production git2/CLI implementation:
   - `RealGitRepository` struct wrapping git2 with async-safe Mutex
   - `open()` and `discover()` constructors
   - Hybrid approach: git2 for local ops, git CLI subprocess for network ops
   - All trait methods implemented with comprehensive test suite (30+ tests):
     - Tests cover repo identity, status (empty/untracked/staged), branching, blame, log, commits, stash, index manipulation
     - Helper: `map_git2_status()` converts git2::Status flags to cupel FileStatus
     - Helper: `run_git()` spawns git CLI with env vars

5. **`fake_repo.rs`** (200+ lines, partial read) - Test double:
   - `FakeGitRepository` - In-memory HashMap-based implementation
   - No real filesystem or git repo needed
   - `set_head_content()`, `set_index_content()`, `set_worktree_content()` for test setup
   - Derives status by comparing head/index/worktree in-memory state
   - Status logic: index = compare index vs head, worktree = compare worktree vs index

6. **`repo_entity.rs`** (150+ lines, partial read) - GPUI entity wrapper:
   - `RepositoryEvent` enum: StatusChanged, IndexChanged, CommitCompleted, BranchChanged
   - `PendingOp` enum: Staging, Unstaging (for optimistic UI)
   - `RepositorySnapshot` - Cheap clone of repo state (branch, stash_count)
   - `GitJob` enum - Async job queue: RefreshStatus, Stage, Unstage, SetIndexText, Commit, Push, Pull, Fetch
   - `Repository` entity: Owns job queue, wraps Arc<dyn GitRepository>, spawns background worker
   - Implements `EventEmitter<RepositoryEvent>`

7. **`store.rs`** (150+ lines, partial read) - GitStore entity:
   - `RepositoryId(u64)` - Unique ID for repos
   - `GitStoreEvent` enum: ActiveRepositoryChanged, StatusesChanged, CommitCompleted, BranchChanged
   - `GitStore` entity: HashMap<RepositoryId, Entity<Repository>>
   - Methods: `new()`, `add_repository()`, `remove_repository()`, `active_repository()`, `set_active_repository()`
   - Observes and subscribes to Repository entities, re-emitting events
   - First added repo becomes active automatically
   - Implements `EventEmitter<GitStoreEvent>`

8. **`diff.rs`** (100+ lines, partial read) - Diff computation:
   - `SecondaryHunkStatus` enum: NoSecondaryHunk, HasSecondaryHunk, OverlapsWithSecondaryHunk
   - `DiffHunk` struct: buffer_range (u32), diff_base_byte_range (usize), secondary_status
   - `BufferDiffSnapshot` - Cheap-to-clone value type with hunks and base_text
   - `compute_diff_hunks()` - Uses imara-diff algorithm to compute hunks between base and current text
   - Includes utilities like `hunks_intersecting_range()`

9. **`status.rs`** (4 lines) - Re-exports:
   - Exposes FileStatus, GitFileStatus, RepoPath, StagingState, StatusEntry

10. **`mod.rs`** (10 lines) - Module declarations

---

### 3. **Epic Documents** (`.epic/` directory)

   - A complete guide to building macOS apps in Rust with GPUI

1→# Building a macOS App in Rust with GPUI
     2→
     3→> A field guide to the essential primitives, dependencies, and architectural decisions
     4→> required before writing your first line of application code.
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## Prologue: What GPUI Is (and Isn't)
     9→
    10→GPUI is not a cross-platform widget toolkit. It is not a WebView wrapper. It is not
    11→AppKit with a Rust skin. It is **Zed's internal application framework** — a
    12→Metal-backed, entity-ownership-driven UI engine that erases the macOS platform layer
    13→entirely, giving you a single Rust-native model for windows, layout, rendering, and
    14→input.
    15→
    16→The consequence of that design choice is significant: you never write Objective-C, never
    17→touch Cocoa, never reach for `winit` or `glutin`. You write `Entity<T>`, implement
    18→`Render`, call `cx.notify()`, and GPUI handles the rest.
    19→
    20→The trade-off is equally significant: GPUI is **not a stable public API**. It is an
    21→internal tool published openly because Zed itself is open source. It breaks on upstream
    22→changes without notice. Pin your dependency to a specific commit and treat forward-ports
    23→as a recurring maintenance cost.
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→## Act I: The Absolute Minimum
    28→
    29→### The Dependency
    30→
    31→GPUI is not published to crates.io. You pull it directly from the Zed monorepo as a git
    32→dependency:
    33→
    34→```toml
    35→[dependencies]
    36→gpui = { git = "https://github.com/zed-industries/zed", rev = "<commit-sha>" }
    37→```
    38→
    39→Pin the `rev`. Never track `main` in a working project.
    40→
    41→### The Entry Point
    42→
    43→The entire macOS run loop is three lines:
    44→
    45→```rust
    46→fn main() {
    47→    App::new().run(|cx| {
    48→        cx.open_window(WindowOptions::default(), |window, cx| {
    49→            cx.new(|cx| MyRootView::new(window, cx))
    50→        });
    51→    });
    52→}
    53→```
    54→
    55→`App::new()` boots the Metal renderer, initializes the Taffy layout engine, and
    56→configures the Cocoa event loop. `.run()` hands control to the platform and never
    57→returns. Everything your application does happens inside that closure or in entities
    58→spawned from it.
    59→
    60→### The Root View Contract
    61→
    62→Every renderable type implements `Render`:
    63→
    64→```rust
    65→pub trait Render: 'static + Sized {
    66→    fn render(&mut self, window: &mut Window, cx: &mut Context<Self>) -> impl IntoElement;
    67→}
    68→```
    69→
    70→Your root view is just a struct that implements this trait. There is no component
    71→lifecycle, no mount/unmount hooks, no virtual DOM. When GPUI needs a frame, it calls
    72→`render()`. When your state changes, you call `cx.notify()` to schedule the next frame.
    73→That is the entire contract.
    74→
    75→---
    76→
    77→## Act II: What GPUI Provides for Free
    78→
    79→You do not wire these up. They come with the dependency.
    80→
    81→### Metal-Backed Renderer
    82→
    83→GPUI owns a Metal render pipeline. Scene construction, GPU resource management, and
    84→frame submission are handled internally. You compose elements; GPUI draws them. You
    85→never touch a shader or a command buffer.
    86→
    87→### Taffy Layout Engine
    88→
    89→Layout is computed by **Taffy**, a pure-Rust implementation of CSS Flexbox and Grid.
    90→The API is Tailwind-inspired:
    91→
    92→```rust
    93→h_flex()                    // display: flex; flex-direction: row
    94→    .gap_2()                // gap: 0.5rem
    95→    .p_4()                  // padding: 1rem
    96→    .child(label)
    97→    .child(button)
    98→```
    99→
   100→`div()`, `h_flex()`, and `v_flex()` are your universal building blocks. Everything
   101→composes.
   102→
   103→### Core Text Integration
   104→
   105→Font loading, shaping, subpixel rendering, and line layout run through Core Text.
   106→You supply font data (via assets — see Act III); GPUI handles the rest.
   107→
   108→### Input Handling
   109→
   110→Keyboard events, mouse events, trackpad gestures, scroll, drag-and-drop — all
   111→normalized and delivered through GPUI's event system. You attach handlers with
   112→`.on_click()`, `.on_key_down()`, `.on_drag()`, `.on_drop()`. The platform
   113→differences between input devices are invisible to your code.
   114→
   115→### Accessibility
   116→
   117→GPUI constructs a basic macOS accessibility tree automatically from your element
   118→hierarchy. No manual `AXUIElement` wrangling required.
   119→
   120→---
   121→
   122→## Act III: What You Wire Up Yourself
   123→
   124→These are not optional. Without them, you have a black window.
   125→
   126→### Asset Loading
   127→
   128→GPUI requires an implementation of `AssetSource` to resolve fonts, icons, and images
   129→by path. The standard approach is to bake assets into the binary at compile time using
   130→`rust-embed`:
   131→
   132→```rust
   133→#[derive(RustEmbed)]
   134→#[folder = "assets/"]
   135→struct Assets;
   136→
   137→impl AssetSource for Assets {
   138→    fn load(&self, path: &str) -> Result<Option<Cow<[u8]>>> {
   139→        Self::get(path)
   140→            .map(|f| Some(f.data))
   141→            .ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("asset not found: {path}"))
   142→    }
   143→
   144→    fn list(&self, path: &str) -> Result<Vec<SharedString>> {
   145→        Ok(Self::iter()
   146→            .filter(|p| p.starts_with(path))
   147→            .map(SharedString::from)
   148→            .collect())
   149→    }
   150→}
   151→
   152→App::new().with_assets(Assets).run(|cx| { ... });
   153→```
   154→
   155→You need at minimum **one font embedded** in your assets. GPUI has no fallback system
   156→font loader. Without a font, nothing renders.
   157→
   158→### A Color / Theme System
   159→
   160→GPUI's styling primitives (`.bg()`, `.text_color()`, `.border_color()`) take `Hsla`
   161→or `Rgba` values directly. There is no built-in theme engine. Zed's `theme` crate
   162→exists but is enormous and tightly coupled to the full Zed workspace.
   163→
   164→Start minimal:
   165→
   166→```rust
   167→pub struct Theme {
   168→    pub background:       Hsla,
   169→    pub surface:          Hsla,
   170→    pub text_primary:     Hsla,
   171→    pub text_muted:       Hsla,
   172→    pub accent:           Hsla,
   173→    pub border:           Hsla,
   174→}
   175→
   176→impl Global for Theme {}
   177→```
   178→
   179→Register it with `cx.set_global(Theme::default())` at startup. Access it anywhere with
   180→`cx.global::<Theme>()`. Grow the palette as requirements demand.
   181→
   182→### The Global Pattern for App-Wide State
   183→
   184→Anything that isn't owned by a specific entity but needs to be accessible from any
   185→context — settings, theme, keybinding registry, feature flags — lives as a `Global`:
   186→
   187→```rust
   188→cx.set_global(Settings::default());
   189→cx.set_global(Theme::default());
   190→
   191→// Accessible anywhere cx: &App is in scope:
   192→let theme = cx.global::<Theme>();
   193→```
   194→
   195→Establish your globals in the `App::run()` closure, before opening any windows. This
   196→is the correct initialization order.
   197→
   198→### Keybinding Dispatch
   199→
   200→GPUI ships a full action dispatch system, but nothing is preconfigured. You define
   201→action types with the `actions!` macro, bind keys explicitly, and attach handlers with
   202→`.on_action()`:
   203→
   204→```rust
   205→actions!(my_app, [Quit, OpenFile, Save]);
   206→
   207→cx.bind_keys([
   208→    KeyBinding::new("cmd-q", Quit, None),
   209→    KeyBinding::new("cmd-o", OpenFile, None),
   210→    KeyBinding::new("cmd-s", Save, None),
   211→]);
   212→
   213→// In render():
   214→div()
   215→    .on_action(cx.listener(|this, _: &Save, window, cx| {
   216→        this.save(window, cx);
   217→    }))
   218→```
   219→
   220→The `key_context` string on your root element controls which action scope is active.
   221→
   222→---
   223→
   224→## Act IV: The Practical Dependency Surface
   225→
   226→Beyond GPUI itself, these are the crates you will need before the project is
   227→non-trivial.
   228→
   229→| Purpose | Crate | Why |
   230→|---|---|---|
   231→| Bake assets into binary | `rust-embed` | Required for font + icon loading |
   232→| Async executor | `smol` | GPUI's executor wraps smol; zero friction with `cx.spawn()` |
   233→| Serialization | `serde` + `serde_json` | State persistence, config, IPC |
   234→| Error handling | `anyhow` | Standard in the Zed ecosystem |
   235→| Ordered tree collections | `sum_tree` | Already in Zed; essential for virtualized list data |
   236→| Compact key-value persistence | `heed` or `rocksdb` | Panel state, user preferences |
   237→
   238→GPUI uses `smol` as its internal async executor. Using `smol` for your own background
   239→tasks means `cx.background_spawn(async { ... })` works without any runtime
   240→configuration.
   241→
   242→---
   243→
   244→## Act V: What You Never Touch
   245→
   246→Because GPUI fully abstracts the platform, these are genuinely off the table:
   247→
   248→- **AppKit / Cocoa** — hidden behind GPUI's platform layer
   249→- **Core Graphics** — GPUI's scene system writes directly to Metal
   250→- **Core Animation** — GPUI owns the display link
   251→- **Objective-C runtime** — no `objc` crate, no `#[objc_method]`
   252→- **`winit` or `glutin`** — GPUI has its own platform window implementation
   253→- **Any JavaScript runtime or WebView** — not in the model
   254→
   255→If you find yourself reaching for any of these, the architectural answer is almost
   256→always to push the work into a `cx.background_spawn()` task and surface the result
   257→back through an entity update.
   258→
   259→---
   260→
   261→## Act VI: The Sharp Edges
   262→
   263→### Platform Support
   264→
   265→GPUI targets **macOS (Metal)** and **Linux (Blade / Vulkan)**. Windows support exists
   266→in the repository but is incomplete. Plan for macOS as the primary target.
   267→
   268→### API Stability
   269→
   270→There is none. GPUI is Zed's internal framework. The context system already underwent
   271→one major refactor (the `ViewContext<T>` → `Context<T>` + `Window` split). The render
   272→trait signature changed. Entity creation APIs changed. This will happen again.
   273→
   274→The mitigation strategy: pin to a commit, do not auto-update, and schedule
   275→forward-port work deliberately rather than reactively.
   276→
   277→### The Mental Model Shift
   278→
   279→The most common early mistake is bringing expectations from React, SwiftUI, or other
   280→retained-mode frameworks. There is no reconciler, no component tree diffing, no
   281→`useEffect`. The model is:
   282→
   283→1. State lives in `Entity<T>` structs owned by `App`.
   284→2. Rendering is a pure function of that state, called every dirty frame.
   285→3. State changes happen through `entity.update(cx, |state, cx| { ... })`.
   286→4. Re-renders are scheduled by calling `cx.notify()`.
   287→5. Cross-entity reactivity is wired through `cx.observe()` and `cx.subscribe()`.
   288→
   289→Once this mental model is fully internalized, the platform layer disappears. You think
   290→in entities, subscriptions, and render functions — and GPUI handles everything below
   291→that.
   292→
   293→---
   294→
   295→## Epilogue: The Initialization Checklist
   296→
   297→Before your first meaningful frame renders, you need:
   298→
   299→- [ ] GPUI pinned to a specific commit in `Cargo.toml`
   300→- [ ] At minimum one font embedded via `rust-embed` and served by `AssetSource`
   301→- [ ] A `Theme` struct registered as a `Global`
